agent_outputs.raw: The draft slide's claim about 'therapy selection across solid tumors' overreaches the evidence in two critical ways. First, the study measures prognostic discrimination — who survives longer under historical treatment — not treatment effect heterogeneity — who benefits more from treatment A versus treatment B. These are distinct scientific questions requiring different study designs. Second, the study population is exclusively early-stage breast cancer at one institution. Extending this to all solid tumors is unsupported — other tumor types have different biology, histopathology, and treatment pathways that make direct generalization invalid without independent validation.

Two scope jumps are bundled in the draft slide's claim. The first is prognosis to therapy selection: predicting survival under historical treatment patterns is a prognostic task; recommending therapy requires knowing which treatment works better for which patients, which requires treatment effect estimation, not prognosis. The second is breast cancer at one center to all solid tumors: this requires validation in each tumor type separately. Neither jump is supported by the current evidence. The retrospective design with confounded treatment assignment further undermines any therapy-selection interpretation.

The oncologists' concerns are valid on both dimensions. The prognosis-versus-treatment-benefit distinction is real and important — a model can improve risk stratification without identifying who should receive which therapy. The cross-tumor generalization is also unsupported — the model was trained and evaluated on early-stage breast cancer slides from one institution, and different tumor types have distinct visual features, biology, and workflows. The appropriate claim is: this model improves prognostic stratification for early-stage breast cancer in similar institutional settings, pending prospective validation.

synthesis: All three assessments identify the same two core scope problems: (1) prognosis is not treatment benefit, and (2) breast cancer at one center does not generalize to all solid tumors. Agents 1 and 2 also raise retrospective treatment confounding. The issues are genuine and the required remedy is a narrowing of the claim and multi-center prospective validation for any therapy-selection claim.

empirical_test.condition: Multi-center prospective or carefully causal studies estimating treatment-benefit heterogeneity within each claimed tumor type
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Model predicts prognosis but not differential treatment benefit; performance degrades when transferred to other tumor types or centers
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Studies confirm the model identifies treatment-benefit heterogeneity beyond standard covariates across claimed tumor contexts
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Model improves prognosis across centers but does not demonstrate regimen-selection value
